label: Negligence
scenario_patterns: A contractor fails to secure scaffolding, causing injury to a passerby on a public road
A doctor misdiagnoses a patient's condition, leading to delayed treatment and worsened prognosis
A building management corporation fails to maintain common property, resulting in a slip-and-fall injury
A driver runs a red light and collides with a pedestrian at a signalised crossing
legal_tests: Duty of care established via the Spandeck two-stage test (threshold factual foreseeability, then proximity and policy considerations)
Breach of duty assessed by the standard of a reasonable person in the defendant's position
Causation established via the but-for test — but for the defendant's breach, the damage would not have occurred
Remoteness of damage — the type of damage must be reasonably foreseeable (Wagon Mound test as applied in SG)
common_fact_patterns: Road traffic accidents involving negligent driving on Singapore roads
Medical negligence claims against doctors and hospitals in Singapore
Occupier's liability for unsafe premises in commercial or residential buildings
Professional negligence by lawyers, accountants, or financial advisors
Construction site accidents due to inadequate safety measures
sg_case_references: Spandeck Engineering (S) Pte Ltd v Defence Science & Technology Agency [2007] SGCA 37 — established the unified two-stage test for duty of care in Singapore
Man Mohan Singh s/o Jothirambal Singh v Zurich Insurance (Singapore) Pte Ltd [2008] SGCA 24 — clarified causation and assessment of damages in negligence
Ngiam Kong Seng v Lim Chiew Hock [2008] SGCA 23 — medical negligence and standard of care for medical professionals
NTUC Foodfare Co-operative Ltd v SIA Engineering Co Ltd [2018] SGCA 41 — duty of care in pure economic loss cases under Spandeck
defences: Contributory negligence under the Contributory Negligence and Personal Injuries Act (Cap 54)
Volenti non fit injuria — voluntary assumption of risk by the plaintiff
Novus actus interveniens — intervening act breaking the chain of causation
Limitation defence under the Limitation Act (Cap 163) — 3-year time bar for personal injury, 6 years for other damage
